journal-title-group.journal-title: The Journal of Veterinary Medical Science

title-group.article-title: Assessment of motor deterioration in a cynomolgus macaque with neuronal ceroid lipofuscinosis type 2 disease using two behavioral analyses

contrib-group.aff: 1)Division of Clinical Medicine, Department of Neurology, Faculty of Medicine, University of Tsukuba, Ibaraki, Japan
2)Department of Veterinary Associated Science, Faculty of Veterinary Medicine, Okayama University of Science University, Okayama, Japan
3)Tsukuba Primate Research Center, National Institutes of Biomedical Innovation, Health and Nutrition, Ibaraki, Japan
4)Laboratory Animal Resource Center, University of Tsukuba, Ibaraki, Japan
5)Department of Molecular and Experimental Medicine, Mie University Graduate School of Medicine, Edobashi 2-174, Tsu, Mie 514-8507, Japan

author-notes.corresp: *Correspondence to: Shimozawa N: shimo@nibn.go.jp, Tsukuba Primate Research Center, National Institutes of Biomedical Innovation, Health and Nutrition, Hachimandai 1-1, Tsukuba, Ibaraki 305-0843, Japan

permissions.copyright-statement: ©2025 The Japanese Society of Veterinary Science

license.license-p: This is an open-access article distributed under the terms of the Creative Commons Attribution Non-Commercial No Derivatives (by-nc-nd) License. (CC-BY-NC-ND 4.0: https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/)

abstract.p: The classical late-infantile form of neuronal ceroid lipofuscinosis type 2 (CLN2 disease) is a fatal lysosomal disorder characterized by progressive motor decline and premature death. A CLN2 cynomolgus macaque (Macaca fascicularis) model closely replicates the late-stage clinical and pathological features of human CLN2. To evaluate motor deterioration in this model, we conducted two behavioral assessments—the CLN2 Macaque Clinical Rating Scale and the Apple test—from the pre-onset stage in one macaque. Hypokinesia was observed as the first symptom at 22 months of age, followed by a marked decline in forelimb movement from 27 months. These simple, non-invasive, and cost-effective tests are valuable tools for monitoring disease progression and may facilitate the future clinical evaluation of therapeutic strategies for CLN2.

body.p: Neuronal ceroid lipofuscinoses (NCLs) are a group of inherited lysosomal storage diseases characterized by progressive motor deterioration and the excessive accumulation of lysosomal ceroid-lipofuscin in the cells of most tissues [6, 25]. One subtype, classical late-infantile neuronal ceroid lipofuscinosis type 2 (CLN2 disease), is caused by mutations in the CLN2/TPP1 gene, leading to a deficiency of functional tripeptidyl peptidase 1 (TPP1) [4, 28]. NCLs have been identified in humans and various domestic animals [7, 8, 11, 13], and CLN2 disease models have been developed in some species [2, 15, 29, 31]. The major clinical symptoms in CLN2 patients include ataxia, seizures, and developmental delay with onset at 2–4 years of age, followed by rapid functional decline and premature death [3, 24, 35].

We recently reported a novel cynomolgus macaque model of CLN2 disease (CLN2 macaque) carrying a spontaneous frameshift mutation in the CLN2/TPP1 gene (CLN2−/−) [19]. The clinical and pathological phenotypes of the CLN2 macaques at the late stage of the disease closely resembled those seen in CLN2 patients and NCL animals, including marked cerebellar and cerebral atrophy [1, 18, 20,21,22]. However, the time course of clinical symptoms in CLN2 macaques has not been investigated. In this study, we applied two behavioral assessments to a CLN2 macaque to quantitatively evaluate its motor functions over time. These methods enabled long-term monitoring and quantification of motor deterioration.

One CLN2−/− male cynomolgus macaque (19 months of age) was used in this study. This macaque was bred and maintained at Tsukuba Primate Research Center (TPRC) at the National Institutes of Biomedical Innovation, Health and Nutrition (NIBN), in accordance with the Rules for Animal Care and Management of the TPRC, the Guiding Principles for Animal Experiments Using Non-human Primates formulated by the Primate Society of Japan [26], and the Guide for the Care and Use of Laboratory Animals [32]. The research protocol was approved by the Animal Care and Use Committee of NIBN (approval number: DSR03-17). The animal was housed in a stainless steel cage (W500 mm × D860 mm × H800 mm) in a breeding room maintained at a temperature of 25 ± 3°C and humidity of 30–70%, with 12 air changes per hour and a 12/12-hr light/dark cycle (lights on from 07:00 to 19:00). The macaque was fed 70 g of commercial feed (CMK-2; CLEA Japan, Inc., Tokyo, Japan) and 100 g of apples daily. Tap water was supplied ad libitum. When the animal showed difficulty with daily activities such as grasping food or debilitation as symptoms progressed, the food was placed in the cage, instead of a feeder attached to the outside of the cage door, for easy access. In addition, under a veterinarian’s management, replacement fluid (Isotonic Sodium Chloride Solution; Terumo Corp., Tokyo, Japan) was administered subcutaneously when dehydration was suspected. Upon exhibiting rapid weight loss, sustained inability to stand, and severe weakness, the macaque was euthanized according to the determination by veterinarians that a humane endpoint had been reached based on the research protocol.

Two behavioral assessments, the CLN2 Macaque Clinical Rating Scale (CM-CRS) and the Apple test, were conducted once a month on the CLN2 macaque from 19 months of age. Prior to initiating the main assessments, a practice period was implemented at 18 months of age, consisting of one session per week for 3 consecutive weeks. During each main testing session, a 15-min acclimation period was provided following both the installation of a clear panel at the front of the cage and the placement of a video camera, to minimize stress and ensure consistent behavioral observation.

We developed the CM-CRS to assess balance while a macaque stands on its hindlimbs, limb tremor, gait, and hypokinesia. This scale is a modified version of existing clinical rating scales for CLN2 patients and for macaques with Parkinson’s disease induced by 1-methyl-4-phenyl-1,2,3,6-tetrahydropyridine (MPTP) [9, 12, 16]. Specifically, our CM-CRS was designed to assess changes of gross motor skills (the abilities to move the arms, limbs, and entire body) and limb tremor over time. All of these four items are scored on a scale from 0 to 3 (0=normal, 1=mild disability, 2=moderate disability, 3=severe disability), yielding a total summed score ranging from 0 to 12. Details of the rating criteria are shown in Table 1Table 1.The criteria of the neuronal ceroid lipofuscinosis type 2 (CLN2) Macaque Clinical Rating Scale (CM-CRS)
CategoryScoreRating criteria
Tremor0Absent
1Tremor present intermittently and fewer than 5 times
2Tremor present intermittently 5 times or more
3Tremor present continuously throughout the observation time
Gait0Normal walking
1Reduced walking speed or frequency of walking
2Walks clumsily, swaying from side to side
3Slips or falls more than once while walking
Hypokinesia0Moves around almost all the time during observation
1Moves around over 50% of the observation time
2Move around less than 50% of the observation time
3Hardly moves at all during the observation time
Balance0Normal, able to stand up, remain standing, and wait until the apple is placed
1Able to stand up only to retrieve apples, then sits down and waits until an apple is placed
2Falls more than once while trying to stand
3Unable to stand up. To complete this rating scale, the activities of the CLN2 macaque in the cage were recorded by a digital video camera placed in front of the cage for 15 min at a time. Prior to recording, the front of the cage was replaced with a clear acrylic panel. No human was present in the animal room where the CLN2 macaque was housed during the recording. To evaluate balance while standing on the hindlimbs, the front of the cage was replaced with one having four holes each with an associated tray at the left and right sides (Fig. 1AFig. 1.
The front of the cage for behavioral analyses. (A) The front of the cage has four holes with small trays at the left and right sides. These trays were used for measurement of the neuronal ceroid lipofuscinosis type 2 (CLN2) Macaque Clinical Rating Scale (CM-CRS). (B) The front of the cage has two holes with elongated trays low down on the left and right sides. These trays were used for the Apple test.). Apples cut into long strips (18 × 5 × 2.5 mm) were placed on each tray, with the goal of encouraging the macaque to stand on its hindlimbs to reach the pieces of apple on the upper trays.

Meanwhile, the Apple test was used to assess the ability of the macaque to move its forelimbs. For this test, the front of the cage was replaced with one having two holes (4 × 5.5 cm) associated with a long tray (1.3 × 4 × 15 cm) on the lower right and left sides (Fig. 1B). Apples cut into small cubes (5 × 5 × 5 mm) were placed in a row from back (macaque side) to front (observer side) on the left or right tray at positions 3, 6, 9, and 12 cm from the front of the cage during one session. The reason for the size of the holes, long tray, and apple is that healthy macaques can easily move their forelimbs through the cage hole while holding the apple. The trays were positioned within the typical forelimb reach range of an adult cynomolgus macaque. The macaque was trained to reach through the hole with a forelimb, pick up the pieces of apple on the tray, and retrieve them. Four sessions were conducted alternately for the left and right sides (total 32 trials). To measure the time from when a piece of apple was placed on the tray until it was retrieved by the macaque, the digital video camera was placed in front of the cage during the test. We defined “errors” as instances when the macaque dropped a piece of apple during one session or was unable to retrieve it within 90 sec. All behavioral tests described above were conducted before feeding to ensure that the animal’s motivation to approach the piece of apple was not diminished. Following each test session, the animal was fed as usual. The behavioral tests were stopped at 35 months of age due to the macaque’s increasingly debilitated state. At 36 months of age, the animal was euthanized in accordance with humane endpoint criteria.

Data of the retrieval time in the Apple test are expressed as mean ± SD. Fisher’s exact test was performed with Bonferroni correction on the error rates of the Apple test. The Shapiro–Wilk test was performed on the retrieval time at each month of age to determine the normality of the data, and then the Steel test was performed. Differences were considered significant at P<0.05. All statistical analyses were performed using R version 4.4.1.

In the CM-CRS, the CLN2 macaque showed no abnormalities between 19 and 21 months of age. However, symptoms started with hypokinesia at 22 months of age, followed in this order by gait abnormality (23 months), limb tremor (24 months), and balance abnormality (26 months). The clinical score of each symptom was initially “1” but gradually increased over time (Fig. 2AFig. 2.
The changes over time in symptom severity as reflected in the neuronal ceroid lipofuscinosis type 2 (CLN2) Macaque Clinical Rating Scale (CM-CRS) scores and the ability to move the forelimbs using the Apple test. (A) The CM-CRS scores for evaluating tremor, gait abnormality, hypokinesia, and impaired balance. Scores of “0,” “1,” “2,” and “3” correspond to normal, mild, moderate, and severe abnormalities, respectively. From 22 months of age, the macaque’s physical activity began to decline (score 1). This became more pronounced from 28 months of age (score 2). By 35 months of age, the macaque was no longer able to maintain a seated position independently and remained lying down throughout the day (score 3). (B and C) The Apple test to assess the ability to move the forelimbs. (B) The error rate significantly increased after 28 months of age compared with that at 19–21 months. (C) The mean retrieval time at 27 months was significantly longer than that at 19–21 months of age. At 32 months of age, there was only one successful trial. From 33 months of age, all attempts to retrieve a piece of apple failed, so retrieval times could not be recorded. Error bars show SD. *: P<0.05, **: P<0.01.). The details of each observed symptom are described below. From 22 months of age, the amount of physical activity began to decrease; the macaque jumped and climbed less, and walked or sat more than before (score 1). From 28 months of age, its activity further decreased to the extent that the macaque hardly jumped and spent a lot of time sitting (score 2). By 35 months of age, the macaque was no longer able to maintain a sitting position independently and lay down all day (score 3). The gait was normal until 22 months of age. Gait abnormality began at 23 months of age, with a slower gait (score 1). One month later, the hindlimbs became unsteady and the gait started to involve jerk-like movements (score 2). From 32 months of age, frequent falls were observed (score 3) and, by 35 months, the macaque was no longer able to walk. Limb tremor was mild at first, and intermittent on the right forelimb and right hindlimb from 24 months of age, followed by tremor of the left limb (score 1). One month later, the tremor worsened to a moderate amplitude and could be observed most of the time (score 2). At 31 months of age, the tremor became severe to the extent that the macaque had difficulties with daily activities such as moving around, grasping food with its hands, and bringing it to its mouth (score 3). The balance while standing on its hindlimbs was normal until 25 months of age. One month later, it was able to stand but its hindlimbs appeared to be weak and it soon sat down, while readily losing its balance (score 1). From 30 months of age, it frequently fell backwards while attempting to stand up to pick up a piece of apple from a high tray (score 2). From 33 months of age, it had difficulty standing up and remained seated most of the time (score 3).

The results of the Apple test are expressed as the sum of those obtained from the left and right trays. Error rates increased rapidly at the later stage of the trial compared with those at the early stage. At 19–21 months of age, the mean error rates in 32 trials were 3.1% (range: 0–2 times), but increased significantly (P<0.05) to 43.8% (14 times) at 29 months (Fig. 2B). Thereafter, the error rates increased significantly (P<0.01) over time, with the macaque failing to retrieve a piece of apple in all 32 trials from 33 months of age. Similarly, the retrieval time (mean ± SD) increased rapidly in the later stage of the trial (Fig. 2C). Specifically, the mean retrieval times at 27 months of age (8.5 ± 9.9 sec) and after 29 months (14.9 ± 13.0 sec) were significantly longer than that at 19–21 months (4.8 ± 4.6 sec), when no symptoms were observed in the CLN2 macaque on CM-CRS (P<0.05 and P<0.01, respectively). Supplementary movie 1 and 2 show some of the behavior during the Apple test at 19 and 29 months of age, respectively. At 32 months of age, only one successful trial was recorded (26.7 sec), although from 33 months, the macaque was unable to successfully retrieve a piece of apple in any of the trials.

In this study, we longitudinally evaluated the motor function of a CLN2 macaque using two behavioral assessments, the CM-CRS and the Apple test, from prior to symptom onset to the end stage of the disease. The CM-CRS was developed to assess the severity of disease-related motor abnormalities in cynomolgus macaques. Similar scoring systems have already been used clinically for human CLN2. To quantitatively evaluate disease progression, such as the Hamburg Scale, which focuses on motor function, visual function, language, and seizures; and the Weill–Cornell Scale, which emphasizes gait, myoclonus, language, and feeding/swallowing [30, 34]. However, the visual and language components of these scales cannot be directly applied to animals. Therefore, we developed the CM-CRS specifically for CLN2 macaques, based in part on a scoring system used for MPTP-induced Parkinson’s disease (PD) model macaques [9, 12, 16]. This system was chosen as a reference because the behavioral symptoms of CLN2 macaques share various features with those of PD macaques, including hypokinesia, gait and postural abnormalities, tremor, and loss of hand dexterity.

In the CM-CRS, the first clinical score was recorded as hypokinesia at 22 months of age. In addition, the severity scores for gait abnormality, tremor, and impaired balance increased from 1 to 2 within 1–2 months of their initial appearance. As these symptoms were initially very mild and progressed in such a short period, routine observational monitoring may have overlooked them. Initiating CM-CRS assessments prior to symptom onset proved effective in detecting early signs of CLN2 with greater accuracy.

In human CLN2 patients, case reports have described that motor symptoms typically begin with gait abnormalities or tremors, followed by falls or balance impairment [14, 17, 35]. The patients’ course of disease generally progresses rapidly, with most of them becoming bedridden within 2–3 years after onset [23], which is equivalent to roughly 8–12 months in macaques. As shown in Fig. 2A, the CLN2 macaque in the current study also showed a pattern of symptom onset similar to that of the patients. In other words, the macaque first showed symptoms at 22 months of age and spent all day lying down by 35 months of age. The time course of clinical symptoms is similar to that of CLN2 patients.

The second behavioral assessment, the Apple test, is suitable for macaques due to their opposable thumbs, which are anatomically similar to those of humans. This allows more direct extrapolation of motor assessment findings from macaques to humans. Notably, Old World monkeys are the only animal species known to replicate human-like manual dexterity, particularly in tasks involving precision grip. For these reasons, the Apple test has been employed in neuroscientific studies using macaques [33]. When the Apple test was performed on three age-matched healthy young macaques in a previous study, they retrieved apples quickly and accurately, within approximately 10 sec. This performance was comparable to that of the CLN2 macaque in the current study at 19–21 months of age, when no symptoms had yet appeared. Additionally, the retrieval time and number of errors (mean: 0) did not increase with age in these previously studied healthy macaques (Ono F., unpublished data). In contrast, the CLN2 macaque in this study exhibited marked increases in both retrieval time and number of errors during the later stage of testing compared with those at the earlier stage, despite the relatively short observation period. The Apple test revealed deterioration of the ability to move the forelimbs in the CLN2 macaque, as indicated by it struggling to pick up small pieces of apple from 27 months of age, in contrast to its relative ease with this task at 19–21 months. In both human CLN2 patients and other CLN2 animal models, motor function typically remains in the normal range from birth until disease onset, after which progressive deterioration occurs [27, 29, 35]. Therefore, performance at 19–21 months of age in the CLN2 macaque, when no abnormalities were detected by CM-CRS, can be considered a hypothetical baseline. The observed increases in retrieval time and errors from 27 months of age onward in the Apple test thus reflect a clear decline in motor function attributable to CLN2 disease. Interestingly, from 24 months of age, the CLN2 macaque retrieved the pieces of apple by gripping them in the palm of its forelimb, whereas prior to this it had picked them up with its fingers. This behavioral change suggested that some abnormalities in the ability to move the forelimbs may have started from 24 months of age and gave us a glimpse of the ingenuity and dexterity that cynomolgus macaques possess.

The Apple test offers two main advantages: simplicity and convenience. Simplicity refers to the ease with which macaques can learn the task within a short period. Indeed, the CLN2 macaque in this study learned to retrieve pieces of apple after only three practice sessions. Convenience refers to the fact that the macaque does not need to be moved to another cage or room for testing. Furthermore, the procedure requires neither surgery nor anesthesia, making it physically less stressful for the animals. For these reasons, the Apple test—being simple, non-invasive, and time- and cost-efficient—was considered optimal for assessing progressive forelimb deterioration in CLN2 macaques.

In gene-modified mouse models for CLN2, some neurological symptoms have been reported [5, 29]. However, these models have limitations in fully replicating the cerebellar symptoms of human CLN2, as they exhibit motor decline and tremors but not impaired balance. The canine CLN2 model has assumed an important role in the development of therapeutic approaches [10]. In this model, neurological examinations, including detailed assessments of gait, posture and seizures, are routinely performed and have provided valuable information. However, in dogs, as a quadruped, the anatomical structure of the limbs differs markedly from that of humans. In contrast, cynomolgus macaques, as nonhuman primates, share key anatomical and functional features with humans, including the ability to walk bipedally and the possession of opposable thumbs. Therefore, behavioral analyses using our CLN2 macaque, the only nonhuman primate model of CLN2 disease, offer a significant advantage in terms of translational relevance, as the findings can be more readily extrapolated to human CLN2 disease.

In conclusion, the motor functions of a CLN2 macaque were quantitatively assessed by the CM-CRS and the Apple test. Future accumulation of data using these two analyses on other CLN2 macaques should deepen our understanding of the natural progression of CLN2 and enable optimization of these analytical approaches, and would be important for assessing the efficacy of future therapeutic interventions for CLN2, such as gene and stem cell therapy.

sec.title: CONFLICT OF INTEREST
sec.p: The authors declare no conflicts of interest associated with this manuscript.

caption.title: Supplementary Movie 1. Digital video data of the Apple test at 19 months of age.

supplementary-material.p: The CLN2 macaque retrieved the piece of apple quickly and accurately.

caption.title: Supplementary Movie 2. Digital video data of the Apple test at 29 months of age.

supplementary-material.p: The CLN2 macaque struggled to pick up the piece of apple and took much longer to retrieve them than at 19 months of age. When the macaque dropped the piece of apple during the session, an “error” was recorded.

ack.p: This study was supported by JSPS KAKENHI Grant Numbers 20K06480 and 23K05613. We are grateful to the staff of the Corporation for Production and Research of Laboratory Primates and HAMRI Co., Ltd. for their careful care of the animals.
